segments.text:
frontalis
occipitalis
temporalis
sternocleidomastoid
masseter
buccinator
orbicularis oris
The temporalis and masseter are 2 of the 4 mm of mastication controlled by CN V III
The other two are the lateral (external) and medial (internal ) pterygoid.
The buccinator (trumpeter’s m) resists inflation of cheeks when blowing out air; holds food close to teeth for chewing
Orbicularis oris = kissing muscle
Frontalis can be used to differentiate stroke vs Bell’s palsy in which the latter affects the frontalis, stroke will spare.
orbicularis oculi
Orbicularis oris and oculi are ring-like bands of muscle that can close an opening so we call them sphincter mm.

Lateral pterygoid
Medial pterygoid
Again, the lateral pterygoid and medial pterygoid are mm of mastication along with temporalis and masseter. By using their other names of external pterygoid and internal pteryoid we can use the mnemonic TIME to remember these mm.

both the coracobrachialis (flexes and adducts arm) and the short head of biceps (flexes forearm or elbow) attach at the same point as the pec minor. This common attachment point is the coracoid process of scapula.

brachialis
Interesting fact: 10% of the population has a third head of biceps which arises at the superomedial part of the brachialis.

Rotator cuff muscles
SITS ALL Morning
S						A
I						L
T						L
S						M
Rotator cuff muscle are important in shoulder movement and glenohumeral stability.
The mnemonic is useful for remembering that supraspinatus , infraspinatus , teres minor, and subscapularis do abduction, lateral rotation, lateral rotation, and medial rotation respectively.

biceps brachii
supinator
pronator teres
brachioradialis
flexor carpi radialis
palmaris longus
flexor carpi ulnaris
flexor digitorum superficialis
bicipital aponeurosis
Layers in the flexor group of the forearm:
superficial -  pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus, flexor carpi ulnaris (all attach to common flexor tendon of medial epicondyle)
intermediate - flexor digitorum superficialis
deep - flexor digitorum profundus , pronator quadratus, and flexor pollicis longus
bicipital aponeurosis- triangular membranous band that runs from the biceps over the cubital fossa; protects structures such as the brachial artery and median nerve.

triceps brachii
3 functional groups of the extensor muscles of the forearm :
1. mm that extend and abduct or adduct the hand at the wrist jt- ECRL, ECRB, and extensor carpi ulnaris
2. mm that extend the medial four digits (fingers)- extensor digitorum, extensor indicis, and extensor digiti minimi
3. mm that extend and abduct the 1 st digit (thumb)- abductor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, EPL
tendons that pass through the extensor retinaculum by compartment:
1. APL, EPB
2. ECRL, ECRB
3. EPL
4. Ext. Dig, Ext. Ind.
5. Ext digiti minimi
6. Ext carpi ulnaris

Quadriceps- 4 headed muscle that extends the lower leg at the knee:
-rectus femoris
-vastus lateralis
-vastus intermedius
-vastus medialis
as the psoas major and iliacus pass the inguinal ligament they merge to form the iliopsoas (our major hip flexor)

Hamstring group- extends thigh and flexes lower leg:
semitendinosus
semimembranosus
biceps femoris (long head)*
*short head of biceps femoris is usually excluded from the hamstring group because it does not attach to the ischial tuberosity and thus does not extend the thigh.
gastrocnemius

tibialis anterior
fibularis longus
fibularis brevis
Fibularis longus and brevis are often called peroneus longus and peroneus brevis. Either is acceptable.
gastrocnemius
soleus
shin splints- often due to swelling and inflammation of the tibialis anterior and its attachment to the tibia- usually due to sedentary people walking or running to far or athletic individuals failing to warm up adequately before long activities.
